title: On Being Translated: A Conversation with a Literary Translator

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: You've translated more than thirty novels into English over a career spanning three decades. I want to ask you something that may sound naive: when you translate, are you writing, or are you doing something else entirely?

body.speaker: Translator
body.text: It doesn't sound naive at all — it's the question the profession keeps circling back to without ever quite resolving. The honest answer is both, and neither. You're writing in the sense that every word on the page is a choice you've made, shaped by your ear, your reading history, your sense of rhythm. But you're not writing in the sense that the imaginative and moral origination belongs to someone else. I sometimes describe it as inhabiting a building you didn't design — you choose the furniture, the light, the atmosphere, but the walls are not yours to move.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: That's a striking image. Does the source author's presence ever feel constraining — as though the walls are too close?

body.speaker: Translator
body.text: Occasionally, yes — and those are the most instructive moments. When a passage resists me, when I cannot find an English equivalent that doesn't feel like a compromise, that resistance is telling me something about the source language that translation can't fully transfer. There is a concept in translation theory — untranslatability — that is often invoked as a philosophical problem, but I've come to think of it as an ethic: when a text resists, the translator's obligation is to make that resistance visible rather than paper over it with a fluent approximation.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: Can you give a concrete example of a moment where you felt you had to make a choice between fluency and fidelity?

body.speaker: Translator
body.text: There's a Portuguese word — saudade — that every translator of Portuguese literature eventually has to confront. It denotes a form of longing for something absent, possibly lost, possibly never fully possessed. The English word 'longing' is etymologically thin by comparison — it gestures at the feeling but lacks the metaphysical weight. Some translators retain the Portuguese word in the text and gloss it in a footnote; others paraphrase; others find an English compound — 'aching absence' or 'tender desolation' — that approximates the texture without claiming equivalence. I've used different strategies at different times. What I've stopped doing is pretending that the problem doesn't exist.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: I'm curious about the relationship with living authors. Does having access to the writer complicate or ease the work?

body.speaker: Translator
body.text: It complicates it, productively. A living author can clarify intention, resolve ambiguities, even endorse a departure from the literal text — which is immensely useful. But it can also introduce a new kind of tension. I've worked with authors who have strong views about how their sentences should sound in English — views that are, frankly, shaped by their imperfect command of the language. Deferring to those views would produce a text that satisfies the author but fails the English-language reader. There's a moment in every collaboration where the translator has to assert that the target language has its own logic, and that fidelity to the source doesn't mean mimicry of its surface features.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: There's been a recent debate about whether translators should receive equal billing with authors. Where do you stand?

body.speaker: Translator
body.text: I think the billing debate is partly a proxy for a deeper question about authorship and ownership, which translation makes unusually visible. What I care about practically is attribution: readers should know who translated a book, because the translator's choices are part of the text they're reading. As for equality with authors — I'm sceptical that the categories are commensurable in a way that makes the comparison useful. What I would argue for instead is recognising translation as a form of intellectual labour with its own craft demands, its own risks of failure, and its own specific contributions to literary culture — rather than treating it as a service industry that produces interchangeable outputs.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: Finally, what do you think is lost in translation that people rarely acknowledge?

body.speaker: Translator
body.text: What is most reliably lost is not vocabulary or idiom but rhythm — the particular cadence of a prose mind moving through thought. Syntax is inseparable from sensibility: a writer who thinks in long, subordinating clauses is thinking differently from one who thinks in parataxis, in short declarative accumulations. English syntax has its own gravitational pull; it wants to do certain things and resists others. So there is always a negotiation between the foreign text's rhythm and English's preferred ways of moving. What translation at its best can do is smuggle in a foreign rhythm, to accustom the reader to a different pace of mind — and that, I think, is one of literature's most quietly radical functions.

annotations.meaningVi: the source of the creative and ethical impulse behind a work — the original author's responsibility

annotations.phrase: inhabiting a building you didn't design
annotations.meaningVi: an analogy for translation: the translator works within a structure created by another, making choices within constraints they did not set
here, fails to yield an equivalent — the source text cannot be adequately rendered without loss
the quality of a word, phrase, or passage that cannot be adequately transferred to another language
to conceal a problem superficially rather than address it honestly
a Portuguese and Galician concept denoting a deep longing for something beloved that may be absent, lost, or unreachable
lacking the depth of historical meaning and cultural associations that a richer word would carry
a brief explanatory note added to a text, often in a footnote or margin
to officially approve a translation choice that differs from the literal source text
yielding to; accepting the authority or preference of someone else
imitation of surface features without understanding or reproducing the underlying meaning
a substitute; a way of debating one issue by arguing about another, less threatening one
crediting the person responsible for a work — here, naming the translator on the book's cover or title page
able to be compared by the same measure or standard
products treated as identical regardless of who made them; something produced by mere technical labour
the rhythm and flow of language — how sentences move and pause
a style in which sentences or clauses are placed side by side without explicit connectives, producing a flat, accumulative effect
a writer's characteristic way of perceiving and responding to the world, as expressed in their prose
an irresistible tendency or force — here, the way English syntax naturally tends toward certain structures
to introduce something subtly without drawing attention — here, to insert a foreign rhythm into an English text
the two competing demands in translation: producing natural-sounding prose in the target language vs. staying close to the source text
producing significant change or challenge without overt confrontation; subtly subversive

grammarNotes.title: Nominalisations to foreground abstract concepts
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Cấu trúc ngữ pháp này xuất hiện nhiều lần trong bài và là đặc trưng của tiếng Anh học thuật trình độ cao. Học viên nên chú ý cách cấu trúc này tổ chức thông tin và tạo nhấn mạnh trong văn phong trang trọng.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The translator uses nominalisations — converting verbs or adjectives into nouns — to name abstract processes as things: 'origination,' 'untranslatability,' 'attribution,' 'negotiation.' This is a feature of academic and intellectual register; it enables complex processes to be discussed as objects, placed in subject or object position, and evaluated.
examples: the imaginative and moral origination belongs to someone else
the translator's obligation is to make that resistance visible
What I care about practically is attribution

grammarNotes.title: Conditional implication without 'if'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Đây là cấu trúc ngữ pháp phổ biến trong tiếng Anh học thuật C1-C2. Việc nắm vững cấu trúc này giúp học viên đọc và viết văn bản học thuật chuyên sâu một cách tự nhiên và chính xác hơn.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Deferring to those views would produce a text that satisfies the author but fails the English-language reader' uses 'would' to convey a hypothetical consequence without an explicit 'if' clause. The conditional is implied by context ('if I were to defer...'). This construction is common in sophisticated argumentation when the condition is already established by the preceding discourse.
examples: Deferring to those views would produce a text that satisfies the author but fails the English-language reader.

questions.prompt: How does the translator characterise the question of whether translation is writing?
options: As a naive question unworthy of serious discussion
As an unresolved question to which the honest answer is 'both and neither'
As a settled debate in which translators are clearly writers
As a question that differs depending on the source language

questions.prompt: The 'building you didn't design' metaphor is used to describe
options: the institutional constraints of publishing contracts
the physical process of working in a foreign country
how translation involves making choices within a structure created by another
the impossibility of translating certain literary genres

questions.prompt: According to the translator, what should a translator do when a text resists translation?
options: Find the closest fluent English equivalent as quickly as possible
Consult the author for clarification
Make the resistance visible rather than disguising it with a smooth approximation
Use a footnote to explain the translation choice and move on

questions.prompt: The translator uses the word 'saudade' to illustrate the problem of
options: how Portuguese literature has been commercially successful internationally
a word that has been mistranslated in most English editions
finding an English equivalent for a word with greater depth and metaphysical weight
why romance languages are easier to translate than Germanic ones

questions.prompt: Some translators retain the word 'saudade' in the English text and add a footnote. This strategy prioritises
options: fluency for the English-language reader
fidelity and transparency about untranslatability
the author's preferred translation approach
commercial appeal to a general readership

questions.prompt: The translator says working with living authors 'complicates it, productively.' What complication does he describe?
options: Authors often demand payment for translation rights that exceeds the translator's fee
Authors whose English is imperfect sometimes insist on translation choices that serve them but fail the reader
Living authors frequently change their minds about their intentions
Publishers restrict direct communication between authors and translators

questions.prompt: What does the translator say fidelity to the source language does NOT mean?
options: Capturing the author's intended emotional tone
Mimicry of the source text's surface features
Preserving the grammatical structure wherever possible
Consulting the author at every difficult passage

questions.prompt: On the billing debate, what does the translator say he actually cares about?
options: Receiving equal credit to the author on the book cover
Attribution — readers knowing who translated the book
Higher royalty payments for translators
A separate translator's note in every translated book

questions.prompt: The translator is sceptical of comparing translators to authors because he thinks
options: translators are clearly inferior to authors in their contribution
the two categories are not commensurable by a single standard
translation will eventually be replaced by machine translation
authors hold the copyright and should always have precedence

questions.prompt: The translator argues translation should be recognised as intellectual labour rather than
options: a form of creative writing
a service industry producing interchangeable outputs
an academic discipline studied at universities
an artistic collaboration between two equals

questions.prompt: What does the translator say is most reliably lost in translation?
options: Specific vocabulary items with no English equivalent
Cultural references that require footnotes
Rhythm — the particular cadence of a prose mind moving through thought
The author's personal voice and point of view
a long subordinating clause structure typical of German prose
a style using short declarative sentences placed side by side without connectives
the technique of inserting untranslated words into a translated text
the process of glossing difficult vocabulary in footnotes

questions.prompt: What does the translator mean by English syntax having its own 'gravitational pull'?
options: English grammar rules are stricter than those of most other languages
English naturally tends toward certain sentence structures and resists others
English has borrowed more vocabulary from other languages than any other tongue
English-speaking readers are more sensitive to awkward prose than readers of other languages

questions.prompt: The translator describes 'smuggling in a foreign rhythm' as one of literature's 'quietly radical' functions. This suggests that translation
options: challenges copyright laws in ways that publishers rarely acknowledge
subtly extends readers' ways of thinking by exposing them to different mental rhythms
is more politically significant than original literary composition
produces a kind of language mixing that undermines grammatical standards

questions.prompt: The translator reframes untranslatability from a philosophical problem to
options: an opportunity for creative invention by the translator
a commercial obstacle that reduces sales in target markets
an ethical obligation to make the resistance visible
a reason to avoid translating certain genres

cloze.template: The translator describes his work using the image of {{b1}} a building he did not design — the walls belong to the original author, but the atmosphere is the translator's choice. He frames {{b2}} not as a problem to be solved but as an ethical obligation: when a text resists, the translator must make that resistance visible rather than {{b3}} over it with a fluent approximation. The example of {{b4}}, a Portuguese word for a particular form of longing, illustrates the challenge of finding equivalents for words with deep metaphysical weight. Working with living authors, the translator notes, can be productive but also creates tension when authors whose English is imperfect insist on choices that fail the {{b5}} reader. On the billing debate, the translator argues that what matters practically is {{b6}}: readers should know who translated a text they are reading. He rejects treating translation as a {{b7}} industry producing interchangeable outputs. Finally, he argues that what is most reliably lost is {{b8}} — specifically the {{b9}} of a prose mind — and that translation at its best can {{b10}} in a foreign mental rhythm, which is one of literature's most quietly radical functions.

summary: On Being Translated: A Conversation with a Literary Translator — a C1-level listening lesson.
translationVi: On Being Translated: A Conversation with a Literary Translator: On Being Translated: A Conversation with a Literary Translator — a C1-level listening lesson.
criticalThinkingQuestion: Based on the arguments presented in this lesson, what do you think is the most important implication for how we think about this topic? Are there perspectives or counterarguments that you feel were not adequately addressed?
